coredata.dc:title: Neodymium doped graphene quantum dots/PANI composite for supercapacitor application

Bharadishettar, Naveen
Devi, Naorem Aruna
Gautam, Ashwani
Chauhan, Sandeep Singh
Siddique, Abu Bakar

Satyanarayan, M.N.
K, Udaya Bhat
Akhtar, Waseem
Rahman, Mohammad Rizwanur
coredata.dc:description: The publication presents a streamlined and economical technique for fabricating advanced electrode materials to enhance the energy storage capabilities of supercapacitors (SCs). The focus is on synthesizing neodymium-doped graphene quantum dots (Nd-GQDs) via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MAH) process. This method uses microwave irradiation's rapid heating and efficient energy transfer under low pressure and minimal reaction time. The resulting Nd-GQDs exhibit enhanced electrochemical properties, including increased capacitance and improved charge storage, making this approach practical and effective for advancing supercapacitor technology. An exceptional specific capacitance of 618 F g−1 at a 5 mV s−1 scan rate is demonstrated using Nd-GQDs as the SC electrode material. Due to their high specific capacitance, Nd-GQDs, when combined with polyaniline (PANI), improve the energy and power density of SCs. Nd-GQDs/PANI composites with varying amounts of Nd-GQDs in symmetric SCs are fabricated to demonstrate their promising properties for SC applications. SCs fabricated with 20 mL of Nd-GQDs in the PANI matrix showed a superior specific capacitance of 354 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, while the energy density and power density were 49.15 Wh kg−1 and 2000 W kg−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Polyaniline
Power density
Energy density
Symmetric supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Imaging of Nd-GQDs using HR-TEM along with particle size distribution histogram, b) Nd-GQDs image and the SAED pattern, c) STEM elemental mapping of Nd-GQDs, d) AFM image of Nd-GQDs with roughness, FESEM images of e) PANI, f) Nd-GQDs/PANI1, g) Nd-GQDs/PANI2, and h) Nd-GQDs/PANI3.
a) FTIR spectra of Nd-GQDs, X-ray Photoemission spectra of Nd-GQDs b) complete survey, c) C1s, d) Nd 3d, e) N1s, and f) O1s spectra.
a) X-ray Diffraction, and c) Raman spectra of Nd-GQDs/PANI composites and PANI.

XPS spectra of PANI and Nd-GQDs/PANI2 a) Full survey spectrum of PANI and Nd-GQDs/PANI2 b) C1s spectra of PANI, c) N1s spectra of PANI d) C1s spectra of Nd-GQDs/PANI2, e) N1s spectra of Nd-GQDs/PANI2 and f) Nd 3d spectra in Nd-GQDs/PANI2.

Nd-GQDs 3-electrode measurement a) comparison graph of the cyclic voltammetry, b) fitting curve of the Eq. 1, c) capacitive and diffusive contribution at different scan rates, d) Nd-GQDs CV graph, and e) EIS of Nd-GQD.

SCs 2-electrode measurement a) comparison graph of cyclic voltammetry b) comparison graph of GCD, c) EIS comparison, d) CV of Nd-GQDs/PANI2, e) GCD of Nd-GQDs/PANI2, and f) Cyclic stability of Nd-GQDs/PANI2.
Ragone’s Plot of Nd-GQDs/PANI composite and PANI.
Elemental composition of PANI and the composites.
Elemental composition of Nd-GQDs using XPS.
Evaluation of PANI and Nd-GQDs/PANI Composites Electrical Conductivity.
PANI and Nd-GQDs/PANI composites EIS parameters.

head.ce:title: Neodymium doped graphene quantum dots/PANI composite for supercapacitor application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The publication presents a streamlined and economical technique for fabricating advanced electrode materials to enhance the energy storage capabilities of supercapacitors (SCs). The focus is on synthesizing neodymium-doped graphene quantum dots (Nd-GQDs) via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MAH) process. This method uses microwave irradiation's rapid heating and efficient energy transfer under low pressure and minimal reaction time. The resulting Nd-GQDs exhibit enhanced electrochemical properties, including increased capacitance and improved charge storage, making this approach practical and effective for advancing supercapacitor technology. An exceptional specific capacitance of 618 F g−1 at a 5 mV s−1 scan rate is demonstrated using Nd-GQDs as the SC electrode material. Due to their high specific capacitance, Nd-GQDs, when combined with polyaniline (PANI), improve the energy and power density of SCs. Nd-GQDs/PANI composites with varying amounts of Nd-GQDs in symmetric SCs are fabricated to demonstrate their promising properties for SC applications. SCs fabricated with 20 mL of Nd-GQDs in the PANI matrix showed a superior specific capacitance of 354 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, while the energy density and power density were 49.15 Wh kg−1 and 2000 W kg−1, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing demand for energy conversion and storage, driven by environmental concerns over global warming and fossil fuel depletion, is spurring intensive efforts to develop renewable energy sources, alternative storage solutions, and conversion technologies, with a focus on creating new energy storage devices that offer high energy and power densities, along with longevity, to support the transition to a low-carbon and sustainable economy [1–3]. The commonly used energy conversion devices include batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells [4]. Supercapacitors, or ultracapacitors, stand out among various energy storage devices for their promising potential, offering high power density, long lifespan, excellent reversibility, and safe operation. However, they do have limitations, notably their relatively low energy density [5–7].

A wide spectrum of nanomaterials has been employed in the fabrication of supercapacitors, including carbon-based nanostructures, conducting polymers, metal oxides, and metal-organic frameworks [8–11]. Among these, polyaniline (PANI) is a popular choice of conducting polymer for electrodes, owing to its multiple oxidation states, tunable morphology, and excellent electrical conductivity, making it well-suited for supercapacitor applications [12]. However, side reactions, swelling, and cracking are major limitations of polyaniline, leading to degradation during the charge and discharge process [13]. Several attempts have been made to discover materials that can be combined with PANI to enhance its properties and stability.

Carbon-based materials are advantageous due to their low cost, ease of preparation, and abundance of electrochemically active sites. Their properties are further enhanced when combined with polymers, improving electrical conductivity and enhancing the nanocomposite's electrochemical performance [14,15]. Incorporating carbon-based compounds like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) enhances surface area, creating many active sites for catalytic reactions. The abundant active sites, large surface area, and exceptional electrical properties of graphene sheets combined with GQDs make them highly suitable for use in supercapacitors [16–18]. The capacitive output can be significantly improved by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as they enhance the interface between electrolytes and electrodes. At the same time, their energy edge effects enable a greater variety of covalent and non-covalent functional groups [19,20]. A conventional hydrothermal method usually takes a longer time, on the order of 10–15 hours, to synthesize quantum dots (QDs). On the other hand, the microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MAH) method produces narrow size distributed QDs in much less time. Several reports have highlighted the MAH method's use to fabricate graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and doped GQDs [21–23].

Functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be used in a variety of applications, ranging from biological imaging and drug delivery to optoelectrical detectors, LEDs, and energy-storing devices [24–27]. The addition of rare-earth elements to nanomaterials is one method to improve capacitance behavior [28]. Rare-earth-based materials have garnered recent interest because they enhance the ionic conductivity, structural stability, and electrochemical activity of electrode materials [29]. Lanthanum-doped NiHN//Fe2O3/GNS asymmetric supercapacitors exhibit an energy density of 51 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 825 W kg−1 [30]. Similarly, the combination of reduced graphene oxide and nickel tin oxide doped with lanthanum (La) shows an energy density of approximately 38 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 870 W kg−1 [31]. In a particular case, the inclusion of Ce3+ increased the number of defects in the NiO lattice, causing protons to diffuse more quickly and preventing Ni's electrons from leaving, hence raising the specific capacitance. The increase in specific capacitance is mainly due to the distortion of the crystal lattice [32]. Motivated by this, we have investigated Nd-GQDs as the electrode material for supercapacitor applications.

Several studies have reported the functionalization of GQDs with Nd3+ [33–35] for applications in drug delivery and bioimaging. However, to the best of our knowledge, their potential for supercapacitor applications has not been investigated. In this work, we synthesized Nd-GQDs using the standard microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MAH) method and Nd-GQDs/PANI composites via the chemical oxidation polymerization method, in which Nd-GQDs serve as the filler and PANI acts as the matrix. We thoroughly investigated the electrochemical properties of these materials and applied them to construct symmetric supercapacitors. Our findings demonstrate that the Nd-GQDs/PANI composite is a promising electrode material with immense potential for energy storage applications.

S. 2.1 (Supporting Information) extensively describes the materials used. Nd-GQDs were synthesized by mixing a 16 mM aqueous solution of neodymium (III) nitrate hexahydrate with a 40 mM aqueous solution of glucosamine hydrochloride and stirring the mixture for 30 minutes. This was followed by microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of 5 mL of the solution in a reactor (Monowave 200, Anton Paar, Austria) for 45 minutes at 180°C under 12 bar pressure. The prepared product was centrifuged several times for 20 minutes at 20,000 rpm to remove impurities. The supernatant collected was used for characterization and electrochemical measurements. The electrode preparation for the electrochemical measurements is described in S. 2.2 (Supporting Information).

Nd-GQDs/PANI nanocomposites were synthesized by mixing different volumes of the Nd-GQDs stock solution during the polymerization of aniline. Aniline monomer (1.0 g) was dispersed in a 1 M HCl solution under stirring, followed by the addition of different volumes of the Nd-GQDs stock solutions (0 mL [0 mg], 10 mL [80 mg], 20 mL [160 mg], and 40 mL [320 mg]) to prepare different nanocomposites, named PANI, Nd-GQDs/PANI1, Nd-GQDs/PANI2, and Nd-GQDs/PANI3, respectively. Then, 2.28 g of aqueous APS solution was added dropwise to each sample. The solution was kept under continuous stirring for another 24 hours to allow slow polymerization at room temperature. The product was washed several times with deionized water and ethanol using ultracentrifugation. The product was then dried at 60°C in a vacuum oven to obtain the nanocomposites. The preparation of the symmetric supercapacitor is shown in S. 2.2 (Supporting Information). Further characterization and electrochemical measurement details are given in S. 2.3 and S. 2.4 (Supporting Information), respectively.

Fig. 1a displays the Nd-GQD structure observed via HRTEM. The particle size distribution, shown in Fig. 1a, reveals that the Nd-GQDs have a mean size of approximately 7 ± 2 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, inset in Fig. 1b, confirms the high degree of crystallinity within the Nd-GQDs. The interplanar spacing derived from the SAED data is approximately 0.37 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystallographic plane characteristic of graphene [36]. The presence of neodymium in the GQDs was confirmed through elemental mapping, as shown in Fig. 1c. Topographical analysis using atomic force microscopy (AFM) reveals a consistent size distribution, with a mean height of approximately 2.969 nm ± 1.091 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1d.

The FESEM images of PANI and various Nd-GQDs/PANI composites are shown in Fig. 1(e–h) at 50k magnification. As seen in Fig. 1e, the PANI sample exhibits an agglomerated structure with irregular-shaped morphology, which contrasts with the composite, which has a less porous structure. In Fig. 1(f–h), the Nd-GQDs/PANI composites show notable morphological changes, exhibiting a pine cone-like structure. When 10 mL and 20 mL of Nd-GQDs are added to PANI, there is less accumulation in the structure; however, Nd-GQDs/PANI2 shows a better and clearer morphology compared to Nd-GQDs/PANI1, as seen in Fig. 1(f-g). When 40 mL of Nd-GQDs is added, the structure undergoes a significant change, resulting in poor surface morphology with increased agglomeration compared to the previous cases. This agglomeration likely causes the Nd-GQDs to form clusters in the Nd-GQDs/PANI3 composite, as evidenced by the EDS analysis of the Nd-GQDs/PANI3 composite ( Table 1), which shows that the weight percentage of Nd in the composite increases more than twofold. Therefore, 20 mL of Nd-GQDs in PANI (Nd-GQDs/PANI2) is optimal, resulting in better morphology and a less agglomerated structure.

Based on the Nd-GQDs FTIR spectra (
Fig. 2a), the absorption peaks at wavenumbers 3326 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 correspond to O-H stretching and the CC bond within the graphite structure, respectively [37]. The detected bands at wavenumbers 2015 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 correspond to the molecular bonds of the nitrile and amine groups, respectively [23]. The strong band centered around 773 cm−1 is assigned to Nd-OH [38]. The peaks at 1148 cm−1 and 969 cm−1 correspond to the C-O vibration band whereas the peak at 2882 cm−1 corresponds to the absorption of C–H stretching vibrations [39]. These oxygen functional groups and doped nitrogen are beneficial for structural wettability and conductivity, thus improving electrochemical performances [40]. The Raman spectrum of Nd-GQDs confirms the chemical structure and crystallinity, as shown in Fig. S31 (Supporting Information).

XPS analysis was performed to determine the chemical states of the as-prepared Nd-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(b–f). The XPS survey scan spectrum displays four distinct peaks at 284 eV, 980.3 eV, 532 eV, 400 eV, and 284 eV, corresponding to the C 1 s, Nd 3d, O 1 s, and N 1 s electron orbitals as shown in Fig. 2b. The XPS broad scan C 1 s spectra are deconvoluted into the following: the CO bond at 288.2 eV, the C-O carbon at 285.2 eV, the C-N at 286.4 eV, and C-C/CC carbon at 284.6 eV binding energy (see Fig. 2c) [41,42]. Two spin-orbit patterns with binding energy values of 1005.7 and 980.3 eV, respectively, are seen in the Nd 3d spectrum (Fig. 2d) and are attributed to the Nd 3d3/2 and Nd 3d5/2 components [43]. The existence of Nd as the Nd3+ oxidation state was revealed by the 3d peak position. Two energy peaks at 983.23 and 979.54 eV are identified by deconvolution of the Nd 3d5/2 transition. The steep peak at 983.23 was attributed to Nd2O3, whereas the less intense peak on the lower energy side was explained as electron transfer from the oxygen to the Nd 4 f3 orbital [43,44]. The N 1 s spectra depicted in Fig. 4e could be deconvoluted into four distinct peaks at 398.75, 399.92, 400.8, and 403.78 eV, which can be ascribed to pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, graphitic N, and oxidized N, respectively [45]. The O 1 s spectrum has good resolution, as seen in Fig. 4f, where notable peaks at 532 eV can be deconvoluted to 532.4 and 533.2 eV corresponding to the CO and C-O/C-OH functional groups, respectively [42]. Additionally, elemental composition was determined using XPS as can be seen in Table 2.

The XRD patterns of the composite Nd-GQDs/PANI and polyaniline (PANI) are shown in Fig. 3a. In the pure PANI sample, three diffraction peaks were observed at angles of 15.14°, 20.38°, and 25.4°. These were attributed as the PANI crystal planes (011), (020), and (200), respectively [46]. These peaks, indicating the repeating units in PANI, display periodic patterns of polymer [47,48]. The Raman spectra of PANI are described in section S 3.3 (Supporting Information). The unresolved HCCH and C-C bonds, which correspond to the wavenumbers of 1477 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1, are revealed by the addition of 20 mL Nd-GQDs in PANI as shown in Fig. 3b. The composites become crystalline when Nd-GQDs are added to the PANI matrix, resulting in sharper peaks. However, in Raman spectra of Nd-GQDs/PANI3, the peaks are suppressed compared to Nd-GQDs/PANI2 due to the poor crystallinity and more agglomeration, as already discussed in Section 3.1. This confirms that 20 mL is the optimum amount of Nd-GQDs. The Nd-GQDs/PANI composite shows site-specific interactions, as indicated by these observations, which may improve the composite's charge transfer characteristics.

The changes in the protonation states of PANI upon doping of Nd-GQDs and the presence of neodymium in the PANI matrix were confirmed by high-resolution XPS spectra of the PANI and Nd-GQDs/PANI2 as shown in Fig. 4a. The deconvoluted peaks of the C 1 s spectra (see Fig. 4(b and d)) correspond to C–C at 284.35 eV, C–N/CN at 285.0 eV, C–N+/CN+ (polaronic-type and bipolaronic-type nitrogen atoms) at 285.92 eV and CO/C–O at 287.58 eV [49]. The Nd 3d3/2 and Nd 3d5/2 peaks, with binding energy (BE) values of 1005.47 eV and 981.44 eV, respectively, are responsible for the two spin-orbit features in the Nd 3d spectrum (Fig. 4f) [50]. This demonstrates the presence of Nd³⁺ in the PANI matrix.

When charge transfer occurs between two interacting materials or chemical species, there’s a redistribution of electron density [51]. This shift affects the core-level binding energy of atoms, which XPS can detect as a shift in the peak position creating π-π interactions [52]. For example, an atom losing electron density (oxidation) will typically show an increase in binding energy, while an atom gaining electron density (reduction) will exhibit a lower binding energy. These shifts can indicate electron transfer across interfaces. The changes in the protonation states and charge transfer interaction of PANI and Nd-GQDs/PANI2 were monitored through the N 1 s spectra as shown in Fig. 4(c and e). These are imine (=N–) at 399.55 eV, amine (–NH–) at 400.29 eV, and protonated amine (–NH2 +) at 401.84 eV in the case of PANI [53] whereas imine (=N–) at 399.93 eV, amine (–NH–) at 401.48 eV, and protonated amine (–NH2 +) at 402.39 eV in the case of Nd-GQDs/PANI2.

In the N 1 s spectra, PANI exhibits nearly the same intensity for the peaks corresponding to amine and imine groups, characteristic of the emeraldine base (EB) form of PANI [54]. However, Nd-GQDs/PANI2 shows a significantly lower intensity for the amine peak compared to the imine group. Additionally, in the case of Nd-GQDs/PANI2, there is an increase in the intensity of the protonated amine peak, confirming doping in the PANI matrix. As PANI undergoes doping or oxidative processes, imine peak intensity increases relative to the amine peak, indicating the conversion of amine groups to imine. This reflects a shift in the polymer’s oxidation state and correlates with its conductive properties. The change in the ratio of imine to amine directly correlates with the polymer’s conductivity.

Using galvanostatic charging-discharging, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and cyclic voltammetry in an aqueous 6 M KOH electrolyte, the electrochemical performance of Nd-GQDs was examined. The three-electrode electrochemical test, using Nd-GQDs as the active material, is shown in Fig. 5. At a scan rate of 200 mV/s, the cyclic voltammetry data presented in Fig. 5a illustrate the electrochemical behaviors of GQDs and Nd-GQDs, compared to bare nickel (Ni) foam. The bare Ni foam shows a minimal integrated area, low specific capacitance, and modest redox peaks.

However, when Nd-GQDs are applied to the Ni foam, there is a significant improvement in the integrated area, indicating enhanced electrochemical performance. The redox peak observed for GQDs differs from that of Nd-GQDs, implying distinct electrochemical behaviors between the two materials. Furthermore, Nd-GQDs show notable improvement in the integrated area compared to pristine GQDs, indicating enhanced electrochemical performance. The increased integrated area observed in Nd-GQDs is attributed to the contribution of the 4 f electronic states from Nd which increases the conductivity of Nd-GQDs enhancing the energy storage capabilities of the material. Therefore, measuring the electrical conductivity of the composite is essential to fully understand its performance in supercapacitors (SCs) as discussed in Section 3.3.2.

Furthermore, cyclic voltammetry (CV) allows for the quantification of the capacitive contribution of electrode materials. CV measurements conducted at different scan rates reveal two distinct phenomena: the faradaic current, which arises from the redox reactions occurring within the material (including its surface), and the capacitive current, also known as the double-layer charge contribution [55]. The current response at fixed potential results from the combination of two independent mechanisms: surface capacitive effects and diffusion-controlled insertion processes. This can be quantified by decomposing the total current (i) at a constant potential (V) into its capacitive (k1v) and diffusion (k2v1/2) components, as expressed in Eq. 1, where k₁ and k₂ are independent constants.
(1)
i(V) = k 1 v + k 2 v 1/2

Fig. 5b illustrates the curve fitting of Eq. 1, while Fig. 5c presents the capacitive and diffusive contributions at varying scan rates. The capacitive current contribution increased with the scan rate, rising from 63.95 % to 91.81 % as the scan rate increased from 5 to 200 mV/s. The ion insertion into graphitic layers is classified as a diffusion-controlled process, whereas the adsorption of ions at the edges and surface oxygen functional groups is considered a surface-induced capacitive phenomenon [40].

The Nd-GQDs electrode's cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve at different scan rates, ranging from 5 to 200 mV s−1 throughout the potential window of 100–500 mV, is shown in Fig. 5d. Nd-GQDs exhibit pseudo-capacitance behavior, characterized by two separate redox peaks or reversible redox processes. Additionally, there is a positive effect on charge storage from oxygen-containing groups which contribute to a large additional pseudo-capacitance and improve wettability [56]. Eq. S.1, Supporting Information was used to calculate the specific capacitance (Cs), which was determined through cyclic voltammetry. At scan rates of 5, 20, 50, 100, and 200 mV/s, the specific capacitance values were 618, 466, 344, 253, and 188 F/g, respectively. Nd-GQDs have not been previously studied for use as an electrode in supercapacitor applications. However, when compared to other doped GQDs, Table S1 (Supporting Information) shows that this value is greater than the specific capacitance of GQD-based electrodes reported in previous investigations. These results suggest that Nd-GQDs can operate sustainably over a broad range of currents. The specific capacitance decreases with increasing scan rates, which can be attributed to the restricted ability of electrolytic ions to permeate into the pores of the electrode materials [57,58]. Despite Nd-GQDs not being previously explored for supercapacitor applications, their Cs value exceeds that of reported GQD-based electrodes in earlier studies.

The remarkable electrochemical performance of the Nd-GQDs was investigated using Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) studies. The corresponding series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) have been calculated to be 0.223 Ω and 27.2 Ω, respectively. The excellent electrical conductivity of the electrode materials accounts for the low equivalent series resistance (Rs). A charge transfer resistance between the electrode and electrolyte is shown by the Nd-GQDs clear semi-circular shape in the high-frequency area of the Nyquist plot (Fig. 5e) [59,60]. Furthermore, a linear trend also known as Warburg impedance is seen in the low-frequency zone, suggesting diffusion. The electrode conductivity is responsible for kinetically-controlled electrochemical reaction (charge transfer), and diffusion of ions. However, both the above phenomena are related to each other.

The electrical conductivity of PANI and the Nd-GQDs/PANI composite was determined using the four-probe method [61]. Resistance was measured at 10 mA in the voltage range of −1 V to 1 V. A micrometer was used to measure the thickness of the sheet with a resolution of 1 μm. The Nd-GQDs in the PANI matrix show excellent electrical conductivity as shown in Table 3. Amongst the composite, Nd-GQDs/PANI2 shows better electrical conductivity. The addition of Nd³ ⁺ increases charge carriers; however, at high doping levels, these atoms act as scattering centers, disrupting the regular arrangement of atoms and causing electrons to scatter as they move through the material. This scattering makes it harder for electrons to flow smoothly, which can reduce overall conductivity. Improving electrical conductivity will result in a better energy density for the supercapacitor.

Fabricating symmetric SCs, PANI, and a composite material of Nd-GQDs and PANI were utilized. Fig. 6 displays the supercapacitor's CV, GCD, and EIS measurements in 0.5 M H2SO4. The GCD curve, as shown in Fig. 6(a-b), is symmetrical and has essentially linear straight lines, but the CV curve is distinctly rectangular and has a huge hump. The results validate the existence of the system's pseudocapacitive charge storage [62,63] and electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) [64] capabilities. After comparing the composites and PANI, it can be concluded that the composite material has a higher energy density than PANI.

In Fig. 6a, we can see that the integrated area covered by Nd-GQDs/PANI2 is the highest among all the samples. The specific capacitance was calculated using eq. S.2, Supporting Information for all the symmetric supercapacitors at current density 1 A g−1. The specific capacitance of PANI, Nd-GQDs/PANI1, Nd-GQDs/PANI2, and Nd-GQDs/PANI3 are 206, 281, 354, and 185 F g−1, respectively. The addition of nanomaterials/quantum dots to the electrode material is one way to improve the capacitance behavior because it creates a more electrochemically active site within the composite. In our case, we demonstrated that by addition of 20 mL of Nd-GQDs to PANI results in a better crystalline structure and the highest electrical conductivity, the highest electrical conductivity which is confirmed by Raman spectra analysis and electrical conductivity measurement. As a result, Nd-GQDs/PANI2 shows the best specific capacitance of 354 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. All of them indicate that Nd-GQDs/PANI composites are potentially promising for outperforming pristine PANI in terms of electrochemical properties, marking a significant advancement in composite materials for various applications.

The Nyquist plot in Fig. 6(c) clearly illustrates the different semi-circular shapes of the PANI and Nd-GQDs/PANI symmetric supercapacitors in the high-frequency region that corresponds to the charge transfer resistance. The Rs and Rct values of the Nd-GQDs/PANI and PANI composites are listed in Table 4. We can see that the solution resistance is minimal in the case of the Nd-GQDs/PANI2 which shows the highest electric conductivity and also the specific capacitance. The diffusion rates of ions are also higher in the case of the Nd-GQDs/PANI2 sample as the charge transfer resistance is least in this case.

CV and GCD tests were performed on symmetric supercapacitors composed of Nd-GQDs/PANI2 composites, with scan rates varying from 5 mV/s to 200 mV/s and current densities ranging from 0.5 A g−1 to 4 A g−1, as seen in Fig. 6(d-e) and the specific capacitance values are listed in Table S2 (Supporting information). Furthermore, at a current density of 1 A g−1, the device's energy density and power density were computed using equations S.3 and S.4, Supporting Information. The energy density and power densities are 49.15 Wh kg−1 and 2000 W kg−1, respectively, exceeding the values of the majority of symmetrical supercapacitors reported in another research as shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information). The incorporation of Nd-GQDs into PANI enhances its morphology, resulting in a more crystalline structure. This improved structural quality makes the Nd-GQDs-PANI composite unique and contributes to its superior energy density.

One essential component of the device's effectiveness is its long lifespan. As shown in Fig. 6f, continuous charge/discharge measurements of the Nd-GQDs/PANI2 composite SCs were carried out for a total of 2000 cycles at a current density of 7.5 A/g. With nearly 99 % retention, the data show a notable increase in capacitance retention. In contrast, PANI shows only around 30 % retention after 1000 charge/discharge cycles. This enhancement is attributed to the use of Nd-GQDs in the PANI composite. Fig. 7 also displays the Ragone plot, which compares the power density and energy density of the Nd-GQDs/PANI composite and PANI SCs.

The MAH approach was effectively used to synthesize Nd-GQDs in a controlled manner, resulting in a particle size of 7 ± 2 nm. In the three-electrode setup, the electrode made with Nd-GQDs exhibits an exceptional specific capacitance of 618 F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. Nd-GQDs/PANI and PANI composites were fabricated, and these materials were used to create symmetric supercapacitors (SCs). The symmetric SC made from the Nd-GQDs/PANI2 sample achieves its highest specific capacitance of 354 F g−1 when tested at a current density of 1 A g−1. Under these same conditions, the supercapacitors deliver an energy density of 49.15 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 2000 W kg−1. Additionally, capacitance retention of the SCs after 2000 charge/discharge cycles shows nearly 99 % retention. The enhanced supercapacitor performance is attributed to improved conductive properties, which have been studied through charge transfer and doping interactions between Nd-GQDs and PANI, as analyzed by XPS. The improved crystalline structure and excellent electrical conductivity of the composite, resulting from the optimal amount of Nd-GQDs added to the PANI matrix, are key factors contributing to the increased energy density. The addition of Nd-GQDs to the electrode materials is a promising strategy to improve capacitance behavior and electrical conductivity. When integrated with PANI, Nd-GQDs exhibit a significant boost in specific capacitance and energy density, highlighting their potential effectiveness for energy storage systems.

Sandeep Singh Chauhan: Supervision, Resources. Abu Bakar Siddique: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Naorem Aruna Devi: Supervision. Ashwani Gautam: Formal analysis. Mohammad Muhiuddin: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Naveen Bharadishettar: Software, Data curation. Waseem Akhtar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Mohammad Rizwanur Rahman: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. M. N. Satyanarayan: Supervision, Resources. Udaya Bhat. K.: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Mohammad Imteyaz Ahmad: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their gratitude to the MME and the CRF at the National Institute of Technology Karnataka, Surathkal, for their vital support. Additionally, appreciation is due to AFMM at IISc Bangalore for their contributions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2025.178516.
